revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:会津田島祇園祭]]
'''Aizu Tajima Gion Festival''' {{Special characters|qq=}} {{Infobox event | name = Aizu Tajima Gion Festival | image = Aizu-tajima Gionsai, Oyatai-unkou.JPG | imagesize = | caption = Grand float procession | genre = [[Festival]] | date = July | frequency = Annual }} {{Special characters|qq=}} {{Infobox event | name = Aizu Tajima Gion Festival | image = Aizu-tajima Gionsai, Nanahokai.JPG | imagesize = | caption = Nanahokai procession | genre = [[Festival]] | date = July | frequency = Annual }} '''Aizu Tajima Gion Festival''' (Aizu Tajima Gion-sai) is a festival held from {{ill|July 22|en|July 22|qid=Q2725|lt=July 22|1=July 22}} to {{ill|July 24|en|July 24|qid=Q2721|lt=July 24|1=July 24}} in {{ill|Tajima|en|Tajima|qid=Q414400|lt=Tajima|1=Tajima}}, [[Minamiaizu, Fukushima Prefecture]], celebrating both [[Tadeuga Shrine]] and [[Kumano Shrine (Minamiaizu)]] (Tadeuga Shrine's Gion Festival and Kumano Shrine's annual festival). The festival is characterized by the unique "Otouya" system, which organizes and conducts the ceremonies. Alongside [[Gion Matsuri|Gion Festival]] of [[Yasaka Shrine (Kyoto)]] and {{ill|Hakata Gion Yamakasa|en|Hakata Gion Yamakasa|qid=Q1749262|lt=Hakata Gion Yamakasa|1=Hakata Gion Yamakasa}} of [[:en:Kushida Shrine (Fukuoka)|Kushida Shrine (Fukuoka)]], it is considered one of the [[Three Great Gion Festivals of Japan]]. On [[January 21, 1981]] (Showa 56), it was designated as an [[Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property of Japan]] under the name '''Tajima Gion Festival Otouya Ritual'''. == History == The festival traces its origins to the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|qid=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}}, during the [[Bunji era]] (late 12th century), when the then-lord [[Naganuma Munemasa]] introduced [[Gion Faith|Gion faith]] to the region, enshrining [[Gozu Tennō Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] (牛頭天王須佐之男命) as a guardian deity of his castle. This tradition was integrated into the existing local [[Tadeuga Shrine]] festival. The festival was temporarily suspended during the rule of {{ill|Date Masamune|en|Date Masamune|qid=Q311183|lt=Date Masamune|1=Date Masamune}} over {{ill|Aizu|en|Aizu|qid=Q1205071|lt=Aizu|1=Aizu}}, but was revived in [[Keichō 8]] (1603) when the townspeople petitioned the then-castle steward [[Ogura Yukiharu]], and it was officially reinstated by the local lord [[Naganuma Morisane]]. It was originally called "[[Tenno Festival]]" and was held on June 15. In [[:en:Meiji 12|Meiji 12]] (1879), the festival of the adjacent [[Kumano shrine|Kumano Shrine]] was incorporated into the Gion Festival framework, establishing the current structure. == Organization == The festival is managed by groups known as "Otouya," responsible for conducting the ceremonies. There are currently 12 Otouya groups, each taking turns every year to organize the festival. The selected year's Otouya is supported by the previous year's group ("Watashi") and the next year's group ("Uketori"). From the organizing Otouya group, a "One-Year Chief Priest" (Ichinen Shinshu) is appointed, playing a central role in the festival. == Schedule of Aizu Tajima Gion Festival == * '''July 22:''' Main Festival, Grand Float Procession * '''July 23:''' Nanahokai Procession, Grand Float Procession * '''July 24:''' Taidai Kagura (Great Sacred Dance) Offering == Nanahokai Procession == This sacred procession offers ritual sake, red rice, and mackerel to the gods, symbolizing divine blessings. It is also referred to as "Japan's Greatest Bridal Procession" due to its grandeur. Participants include men carrying sake barrels, women dressed in elaborate attire carrying special offering containers, and musicians playing [[Gagaku]] (traditional court music). The procession culminates in a grand offering ceremony at the shrine. == Grand Float Procession == The festival features four large floats: Hon-yatai (Main Float), Naka-yatai (Middle Float), Kami-yatai (Upper Float), and Nishi-yatai (Western Float). Each float is managed by designated caretakers who organize its movement and performances. * July 7: Fundraising for festival expenses begins. * July 21: Floats are assembled (floats are now permanently stored and do not require annual reassembly). * July 22-23: The floats parade through the town, stopping at designated locations where children perform traditional kabuki plays. * July 24: Floats are disassembled (now stored without disassembly). The floats have a two-part structure: the front serves as a stage for performances, while the rear functions as a dressing and preparation area. == Children's Kabuki == The Aizu Tajima Gion Festival features traditional {{ill|Kabuki|en|Kabuki|qid=Q199701|lt=Kabuki|1=Kabuki}} performances by children. This practice began in the late {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|qid=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}} but was abolished in the early {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|qid=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}} due to concerns over its impact on children's education. It was revived in 1994 after an absence of over 110 years. The actors are elementary to high school students from local and neighboring areas. Performances include: * "[[Ehon Taikōki]] - Amagasaki Scene" * "[[Nanzan Gimin no Hi]] - Kisaburo's Farewell Scene" * "[[Tokitsukaze Hinode no Matsu]] - Shigi Castle Scene" * "[[Ichi-no-Tani Futaba Gunki]] - Suma Bay Scene" == Taidai Kagura (Great Sacred Dance) == Performed at the [[Kagura-den]] (Sacred Dance Hall) of [[Tadeuga Shrine]] on the afternoon of July 24, this dance is conducted by shrine officials and local residents. The performance follows a solemn and silent tradition, relying solely on music and movements. The dance consists of multiple acts representing creation myths, agricultural rituals, and legendary battles, including: * "Purification Dance" * "Heaven and Earth Creation" * "Sword Dance" * "Rice Planting Ritual" * "Ame-no-Iwato Myth" (Heavenly Rock Cave legend) * "Serpent Exorcism" The festival concludes with the "Great Offering Ritual," marking the end of the annual event. == References == * "Aizu Tajima Gion Festival" by Yasuhito Muroi, Rekishi Shunju Publishing. == Nearby Sites of Interest == * [[Hosoi Family Museum]] == External Links == * [http://www.minamiaizu.org/gion/index.html Aizu Tajima Gion Festival (Minamiaizu Town Official Website)] * [http://www.dydo-matsuri.com/archive/2007/aidu/index.html DyDo Drink Japan Festival] * {{Cultural Heritage Database|302|30|Tajima Gion Festival Otouya Ritual|qq=}}
{{translated page|ja|会津田島祇園祭|version=78451711|comment=Imported full ja history}} <!--Aizu Tajima Gion Festival--> <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[ja:会津田島祇園祭]] {{wikidata link|Q11381253}} [[Category:Minamiaizu, Fukushima]] [[Category:Float festivals of Japan]] [[Category:Gion festivals]] [[Category:福島県にある国指定の重要無形民俗文化財]] [[Category:福島県の祭り]] [[Category:素人歌舞伎]]
